title: The DCYFR Delegation Framework: Typed Agent Contracts at Scale
description: How we built a type-safe delegation system that lets agents hand off work with confidence — covering contracts, error propagation, and approval gates.
author: DCYFR Team

title: Context Window Budget Management for Long-Running Agents
description: Strategies for keeping agents within token limits across multi-turn conversations — including sliding windows, semantic compression, and budget guards.
author: DCYFR Team

title: Mobile Agent Approvals with Mosh + Tailscale
description: How we replaced SSH-based mobile terminals with Mosh for lower reconnect rates and faster agent approval cycles from iOS.
author: DCYFR Team
content:
## The Problem with SSH on Mobile

Agent approval workflows depend on fast terminal access. When an agent pauses for human input, the clock is ticking. SSH on mobile — even over Tailscale — reconnects frequently as the app backgrounds or the network shifts. Each reconnect adds friction.

## Enter Mosh

Mosh (Mobile Shell) uses UDP instead of TCP. It maintains a local prediction of the terminal state, so brief network interruptions are invisible. The session persists on the server; the client reconnects seamlessly.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
mosh \
  --server="$(which mosh-server)" \
  --port=60001 \
  --predict=adaptive \
  workbench \
  -- tmux new-session -A -s main
```

## The Stack

- **Tailscale** — zero-config VPN, handles NAT traversal
- **Mosh** — UDP-based shell, tolerates network changes
- **tmux** — session persistence, named windows for approval flows
- **Moshi app (iOS)** — Mosh client with custom keyboard presets

## Pilot Results

We're in week 1 of a 4-week pilot. Early observations: no reconnects during the first 24 hours. Approval latency feels subjectively faster without the reconnect dance.

Full metrics will be published after the pilot concludes on April 19.
